does: This paper introduces ImageReward, a learned reward model that predicts human preferences for text-to-image generation outputs, trained on 137k expert comparisons collected through a systematic annotation pipeline of ratings and rankings. The authors then build Reward Feedback Learning (ReFL), a direct fine-tuning algorithm that optimizes diffusion models by maximizing the reward signal from ImageReward. Both components are evaluated comprehensively: ImageReward outperforms existing scoring metrics in predicting human preferences, and ReFL demonstrates improvements over baseline methods in both automatic metrics and human evaluation, with all code and datasets released for reproducibility.
connects: This work extends recent progress in learning from human feedback, a paradigm successfully applied to language models through methods like RLHF and DPO, to the vision domain of text-to-image synthesis. It builds on prior work in image quality assessment and preference modeling, but systematically addresses the challenge of scaling preference data collection specifically for vision generation tasks. The paper connects to emerging efforts in reward modeling for diffusion models and responds to the need for better automatic evaluation metrics for generative vision models, which has relied on proxy metrics like CLIP score that often misalign with actual human judgments.
enables: This framework opens pathways for iterative improvement of text-to-image models through human preference feedback at scale, analogous to how RLHF has transformed language model development. The work enables future research in preference-driven fine-tuning of diffusion models, multi-objective optimization balancing multiple human preferences, and development of domain-specific reward models for specialized image generation tasks. It also establishes ImageReward as an evaluation infrastructure that researchers can leverage to assess new generation methods, potentially catalyzing progress in the field similar to how learned reward models have accelerated language model research. Future work could explore combining this approach with other feedback mechanisms or extending it to conditional image generation tasks beyond text-to-image.
